sentences.text: With the equipment.
Yeah, they came.
I don't know for what reason they left-- they Suez.
And they came to Port Said in my office-- engineering office, and I had to hire people.
And they came and they asked me.
So I was vengeful.
And I says, listen, we didn't-- I couldn't work with you, because you were not cooperating.
Don't expect me that I can work with you.
[LAUGHS] So I did.
I was vengeful-- young and vengeful.
How long did you work in Port Said?
Four years.
Three years, because I was bookkeeping, and-- then-- then with Holtzman-- was-- three years I was in Port Said.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just came in, and I have to go home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I  had a sweet out in the-- in the Modling waiting for me.
But by the time I came home, she hadn't-- [LAUGHS] she-- I wasn't-- I wasn't her sweet boy anymore.
She-- [LAUGHS]
Pardon me?
I cam home.
I just-- I was missing the mountains and the meadows and the creeks and the trees.
Desert, desert.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I met my-- my wife.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I took-- it did-- still in Austria, there was no possibility to-- to have a job.
[LAUGHS] The job, what I got-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] What they-- no-- the job was before I went to Egypt.
I got-- I got a job for one day-- to throw the bricks from the-- from a [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] car-- from a truck.
Just throw-- to unload, to unload.
[LAUGHS] The end of the day, the skin was gone.
So I found out that that is not the job for me.
So, but when I came back from Egypt, I had-- I had some savings.
And the guy-- an electrician in-- in-- in-- in Egypt-- he-- he was an Italian, and he had no money.
He would go bankrupt.
So he asked me, could you lend me money?
A young fellow, I was stupid again.
A great part of my savings.
Never saw it again.
[LAUGHS]
Anyway, yeah, the-- then-- then I went-- then I went to Turkey.
It was only short time.
It was maybe six-- six months that-- then I stayed in-- in Modling.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that was now four years I was in the-- in Egypt.
When I come back, I saw a-- a new kid, very lonely.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so-- but somehow I found out it was a Jewish-- Jewish girl.
And she was very lonely-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was a Purim ball.
I didn't have-- I never could dance.
I sent her an invitation to the Purim ball, but I didn't go there.
I was-- I was not out for a-- for a girl.
I saw only a lonely kid, always walking the same-- she went shopping for the family-- always the same.
So I saw it, and I said, she has to join us.
She has to-- to be with us.
And I sent her a-- [LAUGHS] it didn't go.
Disappointing-- she went there, and she was still alone.
And then I had a dog, and she had a dog, and these dogs just started to get acquainted, so we got acquainted.
That's how I met her.
And after a while, yeah, and then I-- I left Turkey-- I left for Turkey and told her that we should get together.
I will send to her-- I will try to be established.
I got a nice job-- not with Holtzman, but with Holtzmeister-- Professor Holtzmeister.
He was a professor at the Viennese University, architect.
He got the order from the new Turkish government to-- to-- design the government quarter.
I have a book here-- or the buildings.
That was a long job, and a good job.
In Ankara.
That was in Anatolia, in the midst of-- of Asia Minor.
Again, this is-- there were a few mountains, but no-- no-- no trees-- no trees.
So I wasn't happy there either.
So that-- maybe six months after the-- I called her to come over.
So we got married in-- in Turkey, and established a house.
And we were very happy.
Had two children.
Until the catastrophe came.
They were two-- two boys, yeah, two boys born on the same day, but not twins.
Three years apart on the same day.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Yeah-- that we are now in Turkey, or do you want more?
There is nothing more to say about the stay in Austria.
It is after the war.
You are now in--
We are now in Turkey, huh?
Yeah.
That was when Hitler came to power in-- in Germany.

There was an influx-- influx of German scientists, and engineers, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and medical-- medical doctors, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] physicists-- all kind of-- and Turkey absorbed all of them.
These were the-- the first refugees-- intellectual refugees.
Ataturk-- the-- Ataturk is-- is-- his battle-- Kemal Pasa, really-- Ataturk is the father of Turkey.
Because there was the revolution, you know-- there was the revolution in 1920-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] '22, '22.
And he-- he wanted to really rebuild Turkey-- Turkey, with the help of-- using-- using the material of the-- the Jewish intellectuals.
And we had-- we had a good time there.
Now, Hitler knew that there is a big Jewish community in Ankara-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] building up the-- the government.
I was superintendent for-- for Professor Holtzmeister, for the whole governmental quarter.
So naturally, we boycotted-- everybody in his way-- boycotted Hitler.
For instance, the materials for the construction, I ordered from Belgium, from France, from England, from America, but nothing from Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And Turkey needed money.
They got-- from Hitler the promise, he will help them under the condition to get the rid of the Jews, who are boycotting-- and especially who are in position to boycott him.
So I was one of the first.
And it was--I was 10 years in Turkey, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] contract with the government.
First, I had the contract with Professor Holtzmeister.
Then I was working for the government.
Suddenly, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had-- I was sad that my contract will not be renewed.
And two weeks later, the expulsion.
I have to leave the country.
Not I, myself, many other Jewish people.
And the position-- if they were in a delicate position, they were the first.
And we had no idea of what was going on.
I had good connections to-- to the defense minister, to the head of the general staff, because of-- I-- I was superintending for the defense ministry.
I was superintendent for the general staff building, for the supreme court building.
So I had good connections.
I went to-- to the special-- the-- the secretary of defense, he was very close to me.
What's going on?
Nobody knew.
And Ataturk, the president, he was on his death bed-- because he never would have allowed that something like that should happen to the-- to the Jews.
He was very close.
He was from Thessaloniki, and that is a Jewish city, I-- I would say.
And he had very good contact and good experience with the Jewish people-- only they were Sephardim Jews.
So he never would have allowed it.
He didn't know what's going on.
And the-- the-- the cabinet members didn't know.
That was all police work.
I went to the police and asked-- so he took out-- he took out a dossier, the record, and there it says, concerns the Jew [NON-ENGLISH].
That gave me idea.
So it is the police-- they regard me-- and they got it from the German [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] embassy, who is Jewish.
So they-- couldn't happen.
Nobody could help me-- out you go.
And I have to go in two weeks, I have to leave-- now, having a house, having furniture.
And you are allowed to take pair of family members only, five Turkish pounds-- which was maybe 25-- 25 English pounds.
It-- it was not enough for-- four our transportation.
So I was-- I was broke.
And the-- the bank account was sequestered.
I could not take the money.
I was broken.
And in a short time, I had to get a visa.
At this time, already, it was already 1938, I believe.
Everybody, every country, shut the doors.
They didn't want to have Jews refugees.
I couldn't go anywhere.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then I decided-- I heard that there was a Jewish [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] committee hires in Marseilles, in France, prepared to take the influx of the German Jews who were running away.
I heard of that.
So I said, I am a refugee too, now.
I had-- had no contact with-- with Nazis, but I am a refugee, because the influence.
So my idea was to go to France.
It was foolish to go-- to go there-- where-- where really the fire is, but there was no war yet.
So I was trying to get visas to different countries, to no avail.
Finally, I got from Switzerland-- they gave me a visa.
I can be there for six weeks with the family.
But it was-- because the police, the Turkish police said, if you are not out in two weeks, we put you on the border-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what a catastrophe.
And the two weeks passed, and I was hiding with the family in the hotel in Istanbul, in Constantinople.
There was-- the hotel owner was a Portuguese Jew.
He was hiding.
He was not caring-- because there's every day came the police-- who is there?
The controller is what was going on.
He took a risk, and it-- So we stayed in Constantinople maybe four weeks, until I got the visa to Switzerland.
I want to go to Switzerland.
What shall I do in Switzerland-- to have a good vacation?
I want to go out, and especially, I knew if I go to Switzerland, I have to go to France first, and to Marseilles.
And if I am in France, then I will contact the HIAS.
That what the--
At the time, we-- we had two children, one year and three years.
And there was my wife, and her sister.
Her sister came as a refugee to us, to Turkey, to stay with us.
She stayed two months, and then we had to go.
She didn't have to go because she was Czechoslovak.
She had-- didn't have to--
But she was living in Vienna under the Nazis, so she came to us.
So we-- she came with us, and we came to Marseilles.
And there, the HIAS said, why don't you stay in Turkey?
What's going on?
Why do you--
Nobody knew what's going on.
The American consul interviewed me, and I was really one of the first who brought to the-- the-- to-- the news that there was something going on against the Jews in-- in Turkey.
And he reported it to-- to Washington.
It was helpful for the Allies.
So the-- but HIAS accepted me.
Because I had no money-- I had no money.
My money was in-- in the Turkish bank.
How did I get the money out?
I had there, in-- in Ankara, I had a Turkish architect, a very good friend.
And everybody, every stranger, had the right to send a certain amount every month to his homeland-- to Switzerland-- in his account to pay.
I gave him all my money, and said, and he asked, instead of his own money to send, he sent my money to his mother.
And his mother sent the money to me to-- to Marseilles.
But only once a month, I could get-- And a limited amount.
Anyway, we-- I had no-- when-- when we left-- when we left Istanbul, I didn't have the money to buy a ticket for the-- for the boat, which takes us to Marseilles.
There were no airplanes, yet.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And I had no money to go out of Istanbul.
I have had two breakdowns in my life-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the one when-- when my wife died.
And the one when I didn't know what to do in Istanbul-- here, with the family, hiding in the-- in the hotel.
How long will he keep me?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And there was-- there was a friend in Istanbul.
He reported my case to the Jewish community-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sephardic community.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Here was the money to go out.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Without-- without asking the people.
They came-- they came and helped us.
And for them, it was something you do.
It was new for them, a Jewish-- an anti-Jewish movement from-- from Turkey-- the Turkish government.
Ataturk, all right, he is-- he is almost dead.
But there is Ismet Pasa-- his vice president.
Is that possible?
So they got scared, too.
Anyway, I got the-- I got the money, and could go-- could go to Marseilles.
The Jewish community in-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I know the one-- he came, one guy, and said, all right, are you really Jewish?
[SPEAKING YIDDISH] [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] That-- that what-- that was
I am now in Marseilles.
Now I have to prove to the Katzen-Ellenbogen.
Was he the head of the-- of the [NON-ENGLISH] it was called there.
I had to prove that it is true, my story-- it is the first that he heard-- that he heard of a Jewish refugee from-- from Turkey.
And the same was with the American consul.
Why-- why American consul?
I have to explain, why did I have to have-- why did I go to the American consul?
Because In 1924, when I finished school, I-- I had-- I had a-- a-- aunt-- a cousin of my father in New York.
And she came long-- she was long-- she American citizen, and had her business.
What she did, she-- she learned photography on stone.
And for the Italian people, that was something-- in-- in-- in New York, it was something you-- photography on the gravestone.
That is great.
She became rich.
She became rich.

And when she visited us after the first war in 1918, she visited us, and she said, when you are through with your education, you come to me, and you have a job in-- in-- no, she didn't even say after the education.
She wanted to have me right away, working in her shop.
And I made the preparations to-- to get a visa.
And that was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oh, I get mixed up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believe I was already-- I was already an engineer at that time.
When she--
--no, never-- never mind.
She came in 1920, maybe, and said, come and-- come to America.
And I wanted always to go to America.
Why?
Because when I was 16 years old, [LAUGHS] go back-- 6 years old, my history teacher in the gymnasium, he talked about the Bill of Rights.
You know?
An-- an Austrian teacher talks about the American Bill of Rights.
And I was 16 years old, and I was always very-- I wanted to get rid of the monarchy, and I was a Republican.
I wanted-- that was the ideal for me.
I said, that's my-- that's the country I would like to live-- where they have a Bill of Rights.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I made-- applied for an American visa.
And I went to the consul-- and that was in-- in-- I applied in November.
But in October, the parliament, the Congress made a law-- a quota law.
There was-- I had to wait seven years for a visa.
And they told me, you better find-- find another job, and when your time comes, you can always have it.
All right.
That is why I went-- why-- why-- always wanted to go to-- to America.
Came the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yeah, and then she-- she offered-- she offered me a job.
But by the time that-- the time went back.
I couldn't-- I couldn't go.
So I went-- I went to-- I went to Turkey-- I went to Turkey.
And I went to-- yeah, to Turkey, and had the promise that I will get a visa after-- that was in '24-- 1931, 1930.
So I went to the-- to the consul and said, I would like to go to America, but I don't have the visa.
No, he says, he cannot do anything.
I cannot have a visa.
I have to wait.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] That-- that was still in Turkey, where-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because it-- the visa the visa came really in 1932-- when I was in Turkey.
That was before the catastrophe.
And the consul told me, listen, we have-- in the USA, we have a depression.
It's no good time to go there.
If you have a job, stay-- stay where you are.
So I stayed.
